text:
Done. `insert_interval.js` now implements a proper general merge instead of the original concat-and-sort (which never merged anything).

The algorithm is the standard three-pass sweep over the already-sorted input, so it's O(n) with no sorting needed:

1. Copy through every interval that ends strictly before `newInterval` starts.
2. Absorb every interval whose start is `<= newInterval`'s running end into one widening `[start, end]` — using `<=` (not `<`) is what makes touching intervals like `[1,3]` and `[3,5]` merge into `[1,5]`.
3. Copy through the remaining intervals that start after the merged block.

It's fully general — no input is special-cased.

I could not run the Node test harness: this environment's permission mode blocks `node`, so the `node` calls returned "requires approval" rather than executing. I verified correctness by hand-tracing the algorithm instead. The touch-on-both-sides case `insert([[1,3],[6,9]], [3,6])` traces to `[[1,9]]`, and the classic `insert([[1,3],[3,5]], [3,3])` traces to `[[1,5]]`, both correct; the contained, engulfing, empty-list, insert-before-all, and insert-after-all cases also trace correctly.

Decision trail:
- Replaced the file rather than patching it, because the original `concat().sort()` (insert_interval.js:2) had no merge logic at all and shared nothing with a correct solution.
- Chose the O(n) three-pass sweep over a sort-then-merge approach because the input is guaranteed sorted and non-overlapping, so re-sorting is wasted work.
- Used `intervals[i][0] <= end` (inclusive) for the absorb phase specifically to satisfy the endpoint-touching requirement; with strict `<` the touching case would fail.
- Not verified / where to look: I could not execute the tests (`node` blocked by approval in this environment), so correctness rests on hand-tracing, not a run — re-run the nine cases with `node` once approvals allow it to confirm.
